Review

Advancements in thermoelectric materials: Emerging trends in organic, inorganic systems, and material informatics
=========================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Satheesh Soumya a, K.S.Fatima a 1, S.Lekshmi a 1, S. Govindan Namboothiri a, P.K.Krishnapriya a, Varsha Arun Shreya a, V.S.Harikrishnan a, A. Chithra Mohan b, Hyunjin Joh c, Janardhanan R.Rani d, Varij Panwar e, K.M.Sreedhar b, Jayakumar P f, Shibnath Samanta g, Ji Young Jo c, Gopinathan Anoop a

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.180661](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.180661 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0925838825022224&orderBeanReset=true)

Abstract
--------

Thermoelectric (TE) materials, which convert waste heat into electricity, have garnered significant attention due to the growing demand for sustainable energy solutions. This review comprehensively examines the fundamental principles, challenges, and advancements in TE materials. It emphasizes the approaches and materials employed to enhance TE properties, including electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and Seebeck coefficient, for achieving superior TE efficiency (ZT). The advances in inorganic, organic, and hybrid TE systems are explored and examined in detail. Inorganic materials like tellurides, sulphides, selenides, and oxides are reviewed for their exceptional TE performance over a wide range of temperatures. Conversely, organic materials such as polyaniline (PANI), Poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT: PSS), and poly (3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) are emphasized for potential use in wearable electronics. Moreover, the integration of machine learning (ML) into TE material research is accelerating the discovery and optimization of high-ZT materials by predicting optimal compositions. This review also provides valuable insights into the latest advancements and highlights the role of ML in driving the future of TE systems, including applications in wearable electronics, industrial waste heat recovery, and sustainable energy generation.

Introduction
------------

Everything we do is driven by energy, from powering our homes and businesses to fuelling our transportation and industries. However, the heavy reliance on traditional energy sources (fossil fuels) has led to significant environmental and sustainability issues, and the energy solutions based on fossil fuels cannot meet the world's rapidly growing electrical needs. The use of fossil energy resources inevitably results in the generation of waste heat energy [1], and to overcome this, developing novel, efficient, and sustainable energy conversion technologies is necessary and highly desired. Thermoelectric (TE) devices present a promising solution by directly converting waste heat into electricity, offering a pathway towards more sustainable power generation, and have the potential to revolutionize power generation by efficiently converting waste heat back into electricity [2]. However, one challenge preventing the widespread adoption of TE devices is their low efficiency. Therefore the development of novel efficient materials that can effectively convert waste heat into electricity is highly demanded.

TE materials exhibiting high electrical conductivity, high Seebeck coefficient, and low thermal conductivity can convert body heat into electrical energy, and make them ideal to effectively use the thermoelectric effect in wearable electronics and circuits [3], [4]. Additionally, TE cooling, based on the Peltier effect, provides a solid-state cooling method that generates a temperature gradient when an electric current flows through the TE material [5]. Unlike conventional cooling techniques that rely on mechanical compressors and refrigerants, TE cooling is reliable, portable, and eco-friendly. TE materials, usually semiconductors that are arranged in a module with n-type and p-type elements thermally coupled in parallel and electrically connected, are at the heart of this process. When current passes through the module, one junction absorbs the heat (cold side), and at the other junction, heat is released (hot side). This mechanism enables precise temperature regulation, making TE cooling suitable for applications that include consumer electronics, medical devices, and space technology [6], [7].

As depicted in Fig. 1a, primary energy sources such as thermal and nuclear power plants, factories, natural gas systems, and automobiles waste approximately 66 % of their energy as heat, leaving only 34 % utilized. This highlights the potential of TE generators to convert waste heat into electricity, as governed by the thermoelectric figure of merit (ZT=σ S 2 T/κ), where σ – electrical conductivity, S – Seebeck coefficient, κ – thermal conductivity, and T – absolute temperature [8], thereby contributing to enhanced energy efficiency and sustainability. The key idea is to develop a material exhibiting both exceptionally high electrical conductivity and low thermal conductivity, with a high Seebeck coefficient[9].

The interdependency of σ, S, and κ makes ZT maximization highly challenging. Materials with a ZT greater than 1 are considered competitive with other methods of electricity generation and refrigeration [10]. However, accurately measuring thermal conductivity—especially in thin films—remains a significant challenge, often due to substrate interference during measurements. As a result, the thermoelectric performance is frequently evaluated using the power factor (PF) instead [10], [11]. The PF, given by σS 2, quantifies the amount of electrical power a material can generate for a given temperature difference. In thermoelectrics, the Seebeck coefficient – the material's capacity to generate voltage when exposed to a temperature gradient- plays an important role [12], [13].

The Seebeck coefficient (an intrinsic material’s property) is given by S = ΔV/ΔT. A positive S indicates holes (p-type conduction), while a negative S indicates electrons (n-type conduction) [14]. In thermoelectrics, electrical conductivity (σ) is just as important as the Seebeck coefficient. The σ of a material describes how easily electrons move through it. High σ materials frequently have high κ , which allows heat to move within the material, opposes the expected temperature difference, and reduces the efficiency. The σ of semiconductors is determined using the relation:σ = q (μ n.n + μ p.p)

where q is the elementary charge, n is the electron density, and p is the hole density, μ n and μ p are the mobilities of electrons and holes, respectively [15]. Thermal conductivity (κ) arises from two parallel contributions: the electronic contribution (κ e), which is associated with the material's charge carriers, and the structural contribution (κ l), which pertains to lattice or backbone vibrations. These vibrations can be described by quasi-particles such as phonons, locons, and propagons. From the Wiedemann-Franz Law, the electronic contribution to thermal conductivity (κ e) and electrical conductivity (σ) in metals is proportional to the absolute temperature (T):κ e/σ = L * T

where, **L** is the Lorenz number, a constant for a specific metal at a given temperature.

Heat transmission via phonons often reduces the temperature gradient necessary for voltage generation, limiting the efficiency of bulk materials due to their high thermal conductivity (κ). Moreover, the strong interrelation between electrical conductivity (σ) and thermal conductivity (κ) in bulk systems poses an additional challenge to optimizing ZT. One strategy to overcome the interdependency of σ and κ is to go from bulk materials to nanoscale structures. Nanoscale engineering offers a novel solution by strategically designing structures that decouple these properties. Nanostructuring effectively reduces κ through enhanced phonon scattering at grain boundaries, interfaces, and defects, thereby suppressing heat transport without significantly affecting charge carrier mobility. Therefore, the transition from bulk to nanoscale, where sophisticated structural and compositional tailoring enables the independent tuning of κ, σ, and S, represents a paradigm in the design of TE materials.

This review addresses fundamental principles underlying thermoelectric conversion and provides in-depth examples of organic and inorganic materials employed in TE applications. Furthermore, it explores the unique properties and potential uses of oxide-based TE materials and p-type conductive polymers. By offering thorough explanations and examples, this review aims to contribute valuable insights into various aspects of TE materials research.

Section snippets
----------------

Inorganic thermoelectric materials
----------------------------------

Inorganic thermoelectric (TE) materials play a crucial role in energy conversion technologies due to their ability to directly convert heat into electricity. Over the years, various classes of inorganic thermoelectric materials have been extensively explored, including chalcogenides, skutterudites, clathrates, half-Heusler compounds, etc. Each of these material families exhibits unique structural and electronic properties that influence their thermoelectric performance. Significant advancements 

Organic thermoelectric materials
--------------------------------

When compared to inorganic TE materials, organic materials have garnered much attention owing to their unique properties, including flexibility, high σ, low thermal conductivity, environmental friendliness, and easy processability [100]. Room temperature applications are a perfect fit for organic semiconducting materials since they exhibit stability up to 200 °C. With comparatively less electrical interaction between molecules than covalent solids, organic materials form van der Waals solids.

Inorganic-organic composite thermoelectric materials
----------------------------------------------------

Inorganic-polymer composites, a unique class of TE, offer the advantages of the thermal stability and chemical resistance of metal oxides combined with the flexibility and processability of polymers. These composites provide a promising approach to achieving high TE performance while maintaining affordability and environmental friendliness. Oxides like zinc oxide and titanium dioxide enhance thermal stability and σ, while polymers such as PEDOT and PANI offer tunability and flexibility. The

Material informatics in thermoelectrics: data and machine learning
------------------------------------------------------------------

In recent years, advances in ML and artificial intelligence have markedly improved the analysis of material properties, the prediction of novel materials, and the design of advanced materials [149]. Significant progress has been achieved in various research fields, including lithium-ion batteries [150], [151], magnetic materials [152], perovskite materials [153], [154], and high-entropy alloys [155], [156]. Data collection and analysis
----------------------------

A fundamental challenge in materials informatics is acquiring accurate and comprehensive data from diverse sources. Materials data can be sourced from experimental results, scientific publications, or computational methods such as Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations [159]. Extracting data from scientific literature significantly contributes to materials informatics. For example, a thermoelectric (TE) materials database was developed using ChemDataExtractor 

Feature engineering in material informatics
-------------------------------------------

Feature engineering plays a pivotal role in achieving accurate results. Feature engineering encompasses the processes of selecting, transforming, and generating meaningful input features from raw data to enhance the predictive performance of machine learning models. In material informatics, the features often include descriptors that summarize the chemical, structural, and electronic characteristics of materials, which are then used to predict their properties, such as TE performances. Features 

Machine learning and deep learning models for material informatics
------------------------------------------------------------------

In materials informatics, the journey from raw data to meaningful insight starts with data acquisition through experiments, first-principles calculations (e.g., DFT), and high-throughput computational screening. These datasets capture critical parameters like electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, phonon dispersion, and thermal conductivity. As datasets become larger and more complex, traditional analysis methods become inadequate.

By applying machine learning and deep learning

Challenges in material informatics
----------------------------------

Machine learning (ML) models have markedly expedited the identification and enhancement of thermoelectric materials. However, numerous obstacles persist that impede their widespread adoption and effectiveness. One major limitation is the availability and quality of data. High-fidelity datasets for key thermoelectric parameters—such as the Seebeck coefficient, thermal conductivity, and power factor—are often limited and inconsistent across different sources. Experimental data may be scarce,

Progress and outlook
--------------------

To enhance the thermoelectric ZT, several strategies focus on optimizing the balance between electrical and thermal properties. One important strategy is the use of nanostructures and nanocomposites, which can preserve high σ while reducing thermal conductivity through phonon scattering. ZT is improved by doping with rare earth or transition metals, which maximizes carrier concentration and the Seebeck coefficient. Dislocations and point defects are introduced by grain boundary engineering and

Conclusions
-----------

The efficiency of thermoelectric (TE) materials plays a crucial role in enhancing the performance of TE devices, with achieving high figures of merit (ZT) being a key objective. Various strategies, such as doping, energy band engineering, and micro-nanostructure processing, are employed to optimize the Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, and thermal conductivity, ultimately improving ZT values. enriched_points.expanded_content: Thermoelectric materials convert waste heat into electricity, promoting renewable energy harvesting and reducing reliance on fossil fuels, thus contributing to sustainable energy practices.
These materials are integral to energy-efficient devices, enabling applications in wearable electronics and industrial waste heat recovery, thereby enhancing overall technological efficiency.
By harnessing waste heat, thermoelectric materials significantly decrease carbon emissions, facilitating a transition towards cleaner energy systems and mitigating climate change effects.
